div.p: When a Value is used multiple times, gradients accumulate from all paths.
Expression: b = a + a Both operands reference the same Value object. The gradient of b with respect to a should be 2.
Inside __add__._backward(): python def _backward(): self.grad += out.grad # self = a other.grad += out.grad # other = a (same object!)
General Principle: When a Value feeds into multiple downstream operations, each path contributes its gradient. Accumulation (+=) ensures all contributions are summed correctly.
Total gradient: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial a} = \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial op_i} \cdot \frac{\partial op_i}{\partial a}$$ Each path's contribution is accumulated, not replaced.

ol.li: Same object, multiple references: When a + a is computed, both self and other point to the same Value object.
Each use contributes: Mathematically, d(a+a)/da = 2, not 1. Each occurrence of a in the expression contributes to the gradient.
Chain rule composition: In complex graphs, a single parameter may influence the loss through dozens of paths. Accumulation correctly sums all these contributions.

div.p: python def _backward(): self.grad += out.grad other.grad += out.grad
python def _backward(): self.grad = out.grad # OVERWRITES! other.grad = out.grad # OVERWRITES!
Mathematical ERROR: Expected: 2, Got: 1 Loss of gradient contribution!
